2010.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~20. Mixed. Bakiyev removed (improvement). New democratic constitution (November 2010) — parliamentary republic, first in Central Asia. Multiple parties. But Osh ethnic violence undermined institutional credibility. Transitional government (Otunbayeva).

sources.citation: Kyrgyz Republic, Constitution of November 2010 — first parliamentary democracy in Central Asia

2011.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~25 — highest. Parliamentary system functioning. Multiple competitive parties. Atambayev elected. OSCE positive assessment. Best institutional moment in Kyrgyz history.

2012.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~24. Near-peak. Parliamentary coalitions functioning. Opposition in parliament. Media open. Courts improving slowly.

2013.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~23. Good. EITI compliant — transparency signal. Manas closure managed smoothly. Atambayev. Parliamentary system.

2014.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~22. Good for Central Asia. EAEU accession — external constraint on governance. Parliamentary. But Atambayev increasingly autocratic.

2015.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~21. Slowly declining. EAEU member. Atambayev restrictions on civil society. Parliamentary elections 2015.

2016.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~20. Continuing decline. Atambayev using courts against opponents. Media restrictions. Jeenbekov campaign.

2017.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~22. Improvement — first peaceful democratic transfer in Kyrgyz history. Jeenbekov elected, Atambayev stepped down. Historic milestone for Central Asian democratization.

2018.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~20. Declining. Atambayev-Jeenbekov feud undermining institutions. Jeenbekov using courts against former patron. Institutional quality declining.

2019.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~18. Declining. Atambayev arrested — politicized judiciary. Rule of law weakening. Media under pressure.

2020.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~17. October revolution — parliament stormed, Jeenbekov resigned. Japarov (released from prison) became president. COVID. Institutional continuity disrupted. Japarov not part of established institutions.

2021.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~12. Sharp decline. Japarov new constitution (April 2021) — reversed parliamentary system, restored strong presidency. Eliminated effective parliament. Kumtor nationalized (institutional overreach). Most significant institutional reversal since 2010.

2022.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~11. Continuing decline. Japarov NGO foreign agent law. Temirov journalists deported. Russia-Ukraine — Kyrgyzstan CSTO participation in Kazakhstan. Kyrgyz-Tajik border war. Institutional quality near Akayev lows.

2023.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~10. Serious decline. 2023 media law. NGO restrictions. Japarov dominant party (Manas). Opposition largely suppressed. Courts serving power.

2024.assessment: V-Dem LDI ~9 estimated. Approaching Akayev-era lows. Japarov LGBTQ law, media restrictions, NGO restrictions. FH approaching Not Free. Parliamentary elections 2025 anticipated (already controlled).

2025.assessment: 2025 partial. Trajectory continues toward consolidated authoritarianism. Japarov.

1995.assessment: Low political violence. ~10 deaths. No organized armed conflict. Some inter-ethnic tensions in Fergana Valley (Osh aftermath of 1990 pogroms still felt). Akayev relatively stable. Criminal violence higher than political.

1996.assessment: Very low. ~5 deaths. Stable. Shanghai Five forming — security cooperation. No armed groups.

1997.assessment: Very low. ~5. Kumtor opened. Stable. No armed groups threatening the state.

1998.assessment: Low. ~10. Russian crisis economic hardship. Some civil unrest. Opposition Aksy-style protests beginning. No armed conflict.

1999.assessment: ~150 deaths. Batken crisis August-October 1999 — IMU (Islamic Movement of Uzbekistan) militants invaded from Tajikistan. Armed fighting in Batken Oblast. Kyrgyz military, IMU fighters, some civilians killed. Japanese geologists taken hostage (later freed). UCDP records armed conflict.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Batken: The Unfinished Business, 1999

2000.assessment: ~80 deaths. Second Batken incursion 2000 — IMU again invaded. Fighting in Batken and Kyrgyz Ala-Too. Less intense than 1999 but significant. Kyrgyz forces with Russian and US Special Forces support.

2001.assessment: ~20. Post-9/11 US destroyed IMU in Afghanistan. Batken threat reduced dramatically. Manas opened — security improved. Residual sporadic incidents.

2002.assessment: ~20. Aksy massacre (March 2002) — police killed 5-6 protesters in Jalal-Abad demanding release of opposition politician Bektur Asanov. State violence against protesters. Significant political event.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Aksy Massacre Investigation, Kyrgyzstan, 2002

2003.assessment: ~10. Low. Post-Aksy relative calm. Akayev consolidating. Kant base opened. Pre-revolution.

2004.assessment: ~10. Low. Pre-Tulip. Civil society growing. Some opposition activists harassed. Akayev family enrichment creating anger.

2005.assessment: ~15 deaths. Tulip Revolution (March 2005) — relatively peaceful. ~15 killed during looting in Bishkek (not direct political violence). Akayev fled without using force on protesters (key contrast with other Central Asian leaders). Bakiyev president.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Tulip Revolution violence assessment, Kyrgyzstan 2005

2006.assessment: ~10. Low. Bakiyev post-revolution. Constitution crisis. Some opposition activists arrested. Low violence.

2007.assessment: ~10. Low. Bakiyev authoritarian consolidation. Opposition journalist Alisher Saipov assassinated in Osh (October 2007) — first high-profile targeted killing.

sources.citation: CPJ (Committee to Protect Journalists), Alisher Saipov murdered, October 2007

2008.assessment: ~10. Low. Bakiyev. Opposition suppressed but not killed in large numbers. Targeted harassment.

2009.assessment: ~15. Bakiyev Manas theater. Opposition leader Omurbek Tekebayev (Ata Meken party) attacked and harassed. Some political violence. Global crisis reducing tolerance.

2010.assessment: ~500 deaths (combined). April revolution: ~80-90 killed when Bakiyev's security forces fired on protesters. June ethnic violence Osh/Jalal-Abad: ~400-800 (depending on source) — Kyrgyz-Uzbek pogroms. Most deaths were ethnic Uzbeks killed by Kyrgyz mobs and security forces.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Where Is the Justice? Kyrgyzstan 2010
International Crisis Group, The Pogroms in Kyrgyzstan, 2010

2011.assessment: ~30. Post-Osh slow normalization. Some Uzbek communities in south still targeted by security forces (discriminatory prosecutions). Atambayev elected. Parliamentary system. Violence declining.

2012.assessment: ~15. Low. Parliamentary system moderating violence. Kumtor dispute peaceful. Ethnic tensions in south still present.

2013.assessment: ~10. Very low. Manas closure. Stable. Atambayev.

2014.assessment: ~10. Very low. EAEU accession. Manas closed. Stable.

2015.assessment: ~10. Low. EAEU. Some religious extremism concerns (fighters going to Syria). Government crackdown on radical Islamist groups.

2016.assessment: ~15. Low. Some ethnic tension in south. Organized crime violence. Religious extremism prosecutions. No mass political violence.

2017.assessment: ~10. Very low. Jeenbekov elected peacefully. Atambayev stepped down peacefully. First democratic transfer — model moment reducing violence risk.

2018.assessment: ~10. Low. Atambayev-Jeenbekov feud intensifying (political not violent). Organized crime. Some ethnic tension in south.

2019.assessment: ~20. Atambayev compound raid (August 2019) — police assaulted his Koi-Tash compound, 1 police killed, several wounded. Atambayev arrested. Clashes between Atambayev supporters and police. Most significant political violence since 2010.

sources.citation: RFE/RL, Kyrgyz Police Storm Atambayev Compound, August 2019

2020.assessment: ~30. October 2020 revolution — disputed parliamentary elections, protests, White House (parliament) stormed. ~6 killed during protests/looting. Jeenbekov resigned. Japarov (released from prison) became president. COVID-19 also caused thousands of deaths (not political).

2021.assessment: ~60. April 2021 Kyrgyz-Tajik border war — ~50 killed (Kyrgyz and Tajik soldiers and civilians). Cross-border artillery and drone attacks. Thousands displaced. Ceasefire but unresolved.

2022.assessment: ~120. September 2022 Kyrgyz-Tajik war — most intense. ~100 killed in 2-day fighting. Tajikistan used tanks and aircraft. ~140,000 Kyrgyz displaced. Most violent border conflict in post-Soviet Central Asia. December 2022 border treaty resolved dispute.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan: Avoiding Renewed Conflict, 2022

2023.assessment: ~10. Very low after border treaty. Japarov consolidating authoritarianism. Opposition suppressed but not killed. Some organized crime.

2024.assessment: ~10. Low. Japarov authoritarianism through legal/administrative means (not direct violence). NGO restrictions. Media censorship. Low political violence but structural repression.

2025.assessment: 2025 partial. Low political violence expected. Japarov consolidated.

1995.assessment: ~90% effective control. Kyrgyzstan has complex borders with Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and China with many undemarcated sections. Fergana Valley border areas poorly controlled. Some rural areas in south effectively controlled by local strongmen and clan networks. No separatist movements. Enclaves: Uzbek (Sokh, Shakhimardan) and Kyrgyz (Barak) in each other's territory, unresolved from Soviet times.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Central Asia: Borders, Enclaves, 2002

1996.assessment: ~90%. Stable but imperfect. Enclaves continuing. Border disputes with Uzbekistan and Tajikistan. Mountain areas in Pamirs sparsely populated and lightly administered.

1997.assessment: ~90%. No significant change. Kumtor mine (Tien Shan mountains) operational — state presence strengthened in that area. Fergana Valley border issues continuing.

1998.assessment: ~90%. WTO member but border controls inconsistent. Enclaves. Fergana Valley. Border demarcation with China ongoing.

1999.assessment: ~78% TEMPORARILY. Batken crisis August 1999 — IMU (Islamic Movement of Uzbekistan) militants seized territory in Batken Oblast, took hostages (including Japanese geologists). Kyrgyz military unable to dislodge — Russian advisers helped. Affected ~12% of territory temporarily. Resolved by October 1999 with IMU withdrawal.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, The IMU and the Batken crisis, Kyrgyzstan, 1999

2000.assessment: ~80%. Batken continued 2000 (second incursion). IMU again crossed from Tajikistan/Afghanistan. Kyrgyz forces with Russian/US assistance. Batken region insecure. Enclaves continuing. US Special Forces training helping.

2001.assessment: ~88%. Batken secured after 9/11 — US operations in Afghanistan destroyed IMU significantly. Batken region more secure. Manas opened — US presence. Border security improved. Enclaves continuing but stabilized.

2002.assessment: ~90%. Batken IMU threat reduced. Border control improving. China border demarcation agreement signed (2002) — resolved some disputes. Aksy massacre in Jalal-Abad oblast — local security issue.

2003.assessment: ~92%. Better control post-Batken era. China border settled. Russia Kant base improved security footprint. Fergana Valley enclaves still unresolved with Uzbekistan.

2004.assessment: ~92%. Stable. Akayev strong central control (authoritarian consolidation). South less controlled (Jalal-Abad, Osh clan networks). Enclaves.

2005.assessment: ~88%. Tulip Revolution (March 2005) — temporary chaos. Looting in Bishkek. South (Osh, Jalal-Abad) rose against Akayev first. Bakiyev from south — southern networks reasserted. Central government control temporarily weakened.

2006.assessment: ~90%. Recovering. Bakiyev consolidating through southern clan networks. Enclaves. Northern-southern divide in territorial administration.

2007.assessment: ~90%. Stable. Bakiyev control through patronage. Fergana enclaves. Batken calm. No separatism.

2008.assessment: ~90%. Stable. Bakiyev consolidated. Enclaves. No separatism. Russia-Georgia war no direct impact.

2009.assessment: ~90%. Bakiyev Manas theater. Stable territory. Enclaves. Some drug trafficking corridor (Afghanistan-Russia via Kyrgyzstan) — informal control by criminal networks in border areas.

2010.assessment: ~82%. Two crises: April revolution (Bishkek briefly chaotic) AND June Osh/Jalal-Abad ethnic violence — government lost control of southern cities entirely for weeks. ~400,000 displaced. Uzbek neighborhoods burned. State unable to protect citizens. Worst territorial control since independence.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Where Is the Justice? Interethnic Violence in Southern Kyrgyzstan, 2010

2011.assessment: ~88%. Recovery. Atambayev elected. Osh slowly recovering — checkpoint system. South still tense. Parliamentary government. Enclaves continuing.

2012.assessment: ~90%. Stabilizing. South recovering. Kumtor dispute — state asserted mining rights. Parliamentary system. Enclaves. Border tensions with Tajikistan and Uzbekistan continuing.

2013.assessment: ~90%. Stable. Manas closure — no longer strategic base. Customs Union. Enclaves. Border demarcation with Tajikistan ongoing (never completed from Soviet era).

2014.assessment: ~90%. EAEU accession. Manas closed. Kant. Border tensions with Tajikistan (Vorukh enclave). Enclaves with Uzbekistan.

2015.assessment: ~90%. EAEU member. Stable. Enclaves. Vorukh border skirmishes with Tajikistan (low intensity). Atambayev.

2016.assessment: ~90%. Stable. Enclaves. Border disputes. Jeenbekov election.

2017.assessment: ~92%. Jeenbekov elected. Stable. Some border demarcation progress. Enclaves.

2018.assessment: ~92%. Stable. Enclaves. Atambayev-Jeenbekov feud — no territorial impact.

2019.assessment: ~92%. Stable. Atambayev arrested (compound siege, 1 killed). Territorial control otherwise stable.

2020.assessment: ~88%. October revolution — Bishkek briefly out of government control. Parliament and White House (parliament building) stormed. Rapid stabilization under Japarov. Enclaves.

2021.assessment: ~85%. April 2021 Kyrgyz-Tajik border war — Tajikistan attacked Kyrgyz villages in Batken, ~50 killed, thousands displaced. Kyrgyzstan lost temporary control of border areas. Ceasefire. Unresolved. Plus: Kumtor nationalized.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan April 2021 border conflict displacement

2022.assessment: ~82%. September 2022 Kyrgyz-Tajik war — more intense. Tajikistan attacked Kyrgyz border areas, ~100 killed, ~140,000 displaced. Kyrgyzstan lost some territory temporarily. Ceasefire after 2 days. December 2022 border treaty signed — major breakthrough resolving 40-year dispute.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan Border Conflict September 2022

2023.assessment: ~93% — significant improvement. Border treaty with Tajikistan (December 2022 — 40-year dispute resolved) — greatest territorial clarification in Kyrgyz history. Most of the 978km border demarcated. Some enclaves resolved. Fergana Valley issues with Uzbekistan continuing.

2024.assessment: ~93%. Stable post-treaty. Some Uzbek enclaves still unresolved (Sokh, Shakhimardan — part of Uzbekistan inside Kyrgyzstan). Japarov centralizing security. No major territorial challenges.

2025.assessment: 2025 partial. Stable territorial control. Remaining enclaves issue with Uzbekistan (negotiations ongoing).

1995.assessment: Post-Soviet transitional budget system. IMF structural adjustment conditions require some budget documentation. Budget presented to parliament but public access very limited. OBI not established until 2006. Qualitative assessment - scant transparency.

1996.assessment: Akaev government; limited budget transparency. IMF program requires basic fiscal reporting. Public budget documents not routinely published. OBI not yet established.

1997.assessment: Transition economy; budget transparency minimal. Kumtor gold revenues off-budget management. Limited public disclosure.

1998.assessment: Minimal budget transparency. Government resists detailed public fiscal disclosure. No independent audit body. Pre-OBI era.

1999.assessment: Budget transparency minimal. IMU crisis leads to off-budget military spending. Kumtor revenues not fully disclosed.

2000.assessment: Fraudulent elections and Akaev consolidation accompany limited fiscal transparency. Budget information not routinely published in accessible formats. Pre-OBI era.

2001.assessment: Post-9/11; Manas base revenues (US $10M/year then more) flowing off-budget to presidential slush funds per some reports. Minimal budget transparency. Pre-OBI era.

2002.assessment: Aksy crisis; budget transparency minimal. Government facing fiscal pressure from domestic unrest and donor requirements for reporting. Scant transparency.

2003.assessment: Pre-revolution; minimal budget transparency. Family business empire (Akaev children) intertwined with public resources. Off-budget spending.

2004.assessment: Pre-revolution period. Budget transparency scant. OBI introduced 2006 but pre-2006 data not systematically collected. Estimated scant_lt_20 based on governance assessments.

2005.assessment: Tulip Revolution (March 2005). Transitional government (Bakiyev/Otunbaeva). Budget chaos during revolution. Post-revolution: donors (World Bank, IMF, ADB) pushing PFM reform. Transparency marginally improving from Akaev era but still scant. OBI pilot survey beginning but 2005 score not published.

2006.assessment: IBP OBS 2006 (first survey): Kyrgyzstan scores ~12/100 — 'scant' transparency. Budget documents exist but not publicly available in comprehensive format. Citizens' budget not published. In-year reports not made public. Post-revolution reforms slow. Bakiyev government not prioritizing fiscal transparency.

2007.assessment: Between OBS surveys. Bakiyev consolidating power; constitutional crisis. Budget transparency estimated minimal to scant based on 2006 score and political context.

2008.assessment: IBP OBS 2008: Kyrgyzstan ~12/100 — scant transparency. No improvement from 2006. Bakiyev authoritarian consolidation does not prioritize fiscal openness. Budget documents prepared but not comprehensively published. Off-budget funds (Manas revenues, donor project funds) not fully disclosed.

2009.assessment: Between OBS surveys. Bakiyev re-elected fraudulently. Budget transparency declining as Maxim Bakiyev controls Central Agency for Development (off-budget investment fund). Estimated scant.

2010.assessment: IBP OBS 2010: Kyrgyzstan ~19/100 — still scant but marginal improvement. Revolution year (April 2010) + interethnic violence (June 2010). Provisional Government (Otunbaeva) more open to transparency reform. Donor pressure for PFM improvements. Still scant overall.

2011.assessment: Between OBS surveys. New parliamentary government. Atambaev PM then president. World Bank/ADB PFM reform programs ongoing. Estimated minimal improvement toward 2012 score.

2012.assessment: IBP OBS 2012: Kyrgyzstan ~23/100 — 'minimal' transparency. Modest improvement. Parliamentary system provides more legislative oversight. Citizens' budget published in limited form. In-year reports partially public. Kumtor nationalization debate drives some interest in fiscal disclosure. Still minimal overall.

2013.assessment: Between OBS surveys. Coalition government. Estimated minimal transparency continuing with modest reform progress. World Bank PFM reform program active.

2014.assessment: Between OBS surveys. EAEU accession context. Estimated minimal transparency. Some PFM improvements from donor programs. Approaching 2015 OBS score.

2015.assessment: IBP OBS 2015: Kyrgyzstan ~31/100 — improved to upper 'minimal' range. Progress in PFM reforms (IFMIS implementation). Parliamentary budget oversight improving. Citizens' budget published. Audit institution (Chamber of Accounts) more active. Still significant gaps in in-year reporting and supplemental budgets.

2016.assessment: Between OBS surveys. Constitutional referendum year. Estimated minimal-to-limited transparency. IFMIS reforms continuing.

2017.assessment: IBP OBS 2017: Kyrgyzstan ~36/100 — upper 'minimal' range. Continued gradual improvement. Presidential election year. Budget documents increasingly online. Parliamentary oversight active. Audit results published. Still gaps in supplemental budgets and in-year reporting comprehensiveness.

2018.assessment: Between OBS surveys. Atambaev-Jeenbekov feud. Estimated minimal-to-limited transparency. Jeenbekov not prioritizing PFM reform initially.

2019.assessment: IBP OBS 2019: Kyrgyzstan ~43/100 — 'limited' transparency. Notable improvement. Budget documents published online. Citizens' budget produced. In-year reports available. Legislative scrutiny (parliament actively debating budget). Audit reports published. Atambaev arrest crisis (August 2019) creates off-budget security spending concerns.

2020.assessment: Revolution year (October 2020). Jeenbekov resigns. Emergency spending for COVID + political crisis. Budget transparency disrupted but formal systems maintained. IBP OBS 2021 covers this period. Estimated limited transparency maintained despite upheaval.

2021.assessment: IBP OBS 2021: Kyrgyzstan ~44/100 — 'limited' transparency. Post-revolution Japarov consolidates but formal PFM systems largely maintained. Kumtor nationalization creates some fiscal complexity. Budget documents online. Parliament role reduced under new presidential constitution (April 2021). Slight concern about reduced legislative oversight.

2022.assessment: Between OBS surveys. Kyrgyz-Tajik border conflict September 2022 (emergency spending). Japarov super-presidential powers reduce parliamentary budget oversight. Estimated limited transparency continuing but with some regression.

2023.assessment: IBP OBS 2023: Kyrgyzstan ~41/100 — 'limited' transparency. Slight decline from 2021. Reduced parliamentary oversight under Japarov's presidential system a concern. Budget documents still published. Citizens' budget exists. Audit reports available. Reduced legislative scrutiny lowering score.

2024.assessment: Estimated continuation of 2023 limited transparency. Japarov super-presidential system reducing legislative oversight. NGO foreign agents law restricting civil society budget monitoring. Estimated ~40-41 or slight regression.

2025.assessment: Estimated ~40. Limited transparency. Authoritarian consolidation continuing. Civil society monitoring restricted. Formal budget documents still published but oversight weakening.

1995.assessment: Pre-EITI. Kumtor gold mine begins production 1997. No extractive transparency framework. Soviet-era mining sector. EITI not founded until 2002.

1996.assessment: Pre-EITI. No extractive transparency. Kumtor mine under construction/early production. No disclosure framework.

1997.assessment: Kumtor gold mine begins production (1997). Major gold resource. No EITI. Revenue flows to government not publicly disclosed. No extractive transparency.

1998.assessment: Kumtor production ongoing. Revenue disclosure minimal. No EITI framework. Sidanko (Russian oil) present. No membership in transparency initiative.

1999.assessment: Kumtor revenues. No extractive transparency. EITI not yet founded. IMF Article IV mentions mining revenues but public disclosure limited.

2000.assessment: Kumtor major gold producer. Maxim Bakiyev and government officials profiting from extractive sector. No transparency framework. EITI not yet founded.

2001.assessment: Pre-EITI. Kumtor revenues off-budget in part. No disclosure. EITI established 2002.

2002.assessment: EITI established 2002. Kyrgyzstan not yet member. Kumtor (Centerra Gold) dominant. Some IMF conditionality requires revenue reporting but not public. No membership in EITI.

2003.assessment: Kyrgyzstan not yet EITI member. Kumtor revenues partially disclosed in government budget. Centerra Gold publishes annual reports (TSX-listed). No EITI membership.

2004.assessment: Kyrgyzstan EITI candidate country (2004). Commits to EITI process. Kumtor (Centerra Gold IPO 2004 on TSX) - publicly listed, more disclosure. EITI validation process beginning.

2005.assessment: EITI candidate. Tulip Revolution creates governance disruption. EITI process slowed during transition. Centerra Gold listed, publishes royalty/tax payments to KG government. Candidate status maintained.

2006.assessment: EITI candidate. Bakiyev government nominally committed to EITI. First EITI reconciliation reports being prepared. Gold sector: Kumtor dominant. Some progress in disclosure.

2007.assessment: EITI candidate. First EITI reports published covering 2005-2006 fiscal years. Constitutional instability but EITI process continuing. Limited progress. Some extractive sector companies reporting.

2008.assessment: EITI candidate. Reports covering 2006-2007. Bakiyev government acquiring extractive assets (Maxim Bakiyev Central Asia Gold). Conflict of interest between transparency requirements and family business. Candidate status.

2009.assessment: EITI candidate. Reports covering 2007-2008. Bakiyev era corruption limits genuine transparency even while EITI reporting continues formally. Candidate.

2010.assessment: Revolution year (April + June 2010). EITI process disrupted but provisional government (Otunbaeva) more reform-oriented. EITI candidate status maintained through transition.

2011.assessment: Kyrgyzstan declared EITI compliant (2011). First Central Asian country to achieve compliance. Annual EITI reports covering Kumtor and other extractive companies. Revenue reconciliation published. Meaningful compliance.

2012.assessment: EITI compliant. Regular annual reports. Kumtor nationalization debate brings more scrutiny to extractive revenues. EITI reports publicly available. Multi-stakeholder group active. Meaningful compliance.

2013.assessment: EITI compliant. Reports covering 2011-2012. Parliamentary debate on Kumtor uses EITI data. Multi-stakeholder group (MSG) functional. Meaningful compliance with some gaps.

2014.assessment: EITI compliant. Kumtor controversy drives public interest in EITI data. EITI report covers gold, coal, oil. Revenue reconciliation published. EAEU accession does not affect EITI status. Meaningful compliance.

2015.assessment: EITI compliant. New EITI Standard (2013) implementation. Kyrgyzstan re-validated. Reports timely and comprehensive. Kumtor as dominant revenue source disclosed. Meaningful compliance.

2016.assessment: EITI compliant. Annual reports covering 2014-2015. Political stability improving. MSG continues. Constitutional referendum does not affect EITI. Meaningful compliance.

2017.assessment: EITI compliant. Reports covering 2015-2016. Presidential transition (Atambaev to Jeenbekov). EITI process maintained through transition. Meaningful compliance.

2018.assessment: EITI compliant. Reports covering 2016-2017. Atambaev-Jeenbekov feud creates some uncertainty but EITI process continues. Meaningful compliance.

2019.assessment: EITI compliant. Reports covering 2017-2018. Atambaev arrested August 2019. EITI not directly affected. Meaningful compliance with extractive revenue disclosure.

2020.assessment: EITI compliant. Revolution year (October 2020). EITI reports covering 2018-2019. Kumtor nationalization debate intensifies. EITI data used in Kumtor legal proceedings. Meaningful compliance.

2021.assessment: EITI compliant. Kumtor nationalized (2021). Japarov government. Nationalized Kyrgyzaltyn now dominant. EITI process continues for nationalized entity. Some uncertainty about disclosure quality post-nationalization. Meaningful compliance.

2022.assessment: EITI compliant. Post-Kumtor nationalization reports. Kyrgyzaltyn (state company) integrated into EITI reporting. Some concerns about quality of state company disclosure. Corrective actions possible.

2023.assessment: EITI compliant. Reports covering 2021-2022. Post-nationalization extractive sector dominated by state. MSG continues. Some concern about political pressure on EITI disclosure given Japarov authoritarian consolidation. Meaningful but with concerns.

2024.assessment: Estimated EITI compliant. Japarov government. NGO foreign agents law (2022) restricts civil society MSG members. Some risk to EITI compliance if MSG civil society participation undermined. Estimated meaningful compliance.

2025.assessment: Estimated EITI compliant. Foreign agents law risk to EITI civil society component. But formal compliance maintained per last available data. Estimated meaningful compliance with growing concerns.

1995.assessment: Akaev era. Multiple independent newspapers active including Res Publica. Government uses defamation laws, advertising pressure, printing press control. Broadcast media state-dominated. Regional media poor. RSF not yet established. Estimated equivalent to difficult category.

1996.assessment: Akaev island of democracy rhetoric fading. Independent press (Res Publica) facing increasing pressure. Government using printing monopoly and defamation suits. Difficult environment.

1997.assessment: Press restrictions continuing. Independent journalists face defamation charges. TV broadcast dominated by state. Some FM radio emerging. CPJ documents incidents. Estimated difficult.

1998.assessment: Res Publica editor Zamira Sydykova jailed (1997-1999) on criminal defamation charges. Major press freedom crisis. Government using courts against critics. Estimated very_serious due to editor imprisonment.

sources.citation: Committee to Protect Journalists, Sydykova case, 1998

1999.assessment: Sydykova released (1999) after international pressure but press freedom still very serious. IMU incursion - journalists banned from Batken operations. Media covering opposition increasingly targeted. Estimated very_serious.

2000.assessment: Fraudulent elections (parliamentary and presidential). Independent media coverage of fraud restricted. Felix Kulov denied access to media during election campaign. Journalists self-censoring. Press environment very serious. RSF launches index in 2002 but this period estimated.

2001.assessment: Post-9/11 context. Independent media continuing under pressure. Manas base agreement restricts coverage of military activities. Aksy protests begin - coverage limited. CPJ documents cases. Estimated very_serious.

2002.assessment: First RSF ranking (2002): Kyrgyzstan ~101/139. Aksy shootings (March 2002) - journalists filming protest killings harassed. Beknazarov coverage restricted. Despite repression, Kyrgyzstan had relatively active independent press for Central Asia. Environment: difficult.

2003.assessment: RSF ~98. Pre-revolution; multiple independent newspapers active. Government pressure through defamation suits. Akaev using courts against critical media. Difficult environment.

2004.assessment: RSF ~107. Pre-revolution opposition media mobilizing. Akaev family (children Aydar and Bermet) acquire media outlets. Opposition media (Moya Stolitsa Novosti) very active. Difficult environment.

2005.assessment: RSF ~109. Tulip Revolution (March 2005): independent media crucial in revolution coverage. Post-revolution: brief press freedom improvement. But RSF notes overall difficult environment given pre-revolution repression.

2006.assessment: RSF ~111. Post-revolution freedoms eroding. Bakiyev consolidating. Journalists covering opposition protests (November 2006) face pressure. Some media outlets bought by pro-Bakiyev oligarchs. Difficult environment.

2007.assessment: RSF ~110. Bakiyev dismisses Kulov (January 2007). Journalists facing increasing threats. Fraudulent parliamentary elections (December 2007) - media coverage restricted. Difficult environment.

2008.assessment: RSF ~111. Bakiyev authoritarian consolidation. Independent journalists face physical threats. Maxim Bakiyev controls major media outlets. Self-censorship endemic. Difficult environment.

2009.assessment: RSF ~125 - sharp decline. Bakiyev fraudulent re-election. Independent coverage suppressed. Journalist Gennady Pavlyuk killed 2009 (thrown from window). Very serious environment for independent journalists.

2010.assessment: RSF ~108. April 2010 revolution - journalists covering protests targeted by Bakiyev security forces. June 2010 interethnic violence: media covering ethnic tensions at extreme risk. Uzbek-language media targeted. Post-revolution Otunbaeva government more press-friendly. Difficult year overall.

2011.assessment: RSF ~106. Post-revolution parliamentary system. Atambaev-era press environment more open than Bakiyev. Independent media growing. Online media expanding. But self-censorship on ethnic issues. Difficult environment improving.

2012.assessment: RSF ~106. Parliamentary system provides some protection for press freedom. Multiple independent outlets active. Kloop.kg, 24.kg operating. Ethnic/south coverage sensitive. Difficult environment.

2013.assessment: RSF ~106. Atambaev government; relatively open press environment for Central Asia. Kloop.kg, 24.kg, Next.kg, Zanoza.kg active online media. Some self-censorship. Journalist Pavlyuk murder unpunished. Difficult environment broadly.

2014.assessment: RSF ~88 - improvement. RSF notes Kyrgyzstan as most press-free in Central Asia under parliamentary system. Multiple independent media. Online media growing rapidly. Journalists covering Kumtor controversy active. Problematic environment.

2015.assessment: RSF ~85. Kyrgyzstan maintains relative press freedom vs. Central Asian neighbors. Kloop.kg, Azattyk, 24.kg active. Some journalist harassment. Self-censorship on politically sensitive topics. Problematic.

2016.assessment: RSF ~85. Atambaev government maintains relatively open press environment by regional standards. Online media vigorous. Constitutional referendum covered by independent media. Some self-censorship. Problematic.

2017.assessment: RSF ~86. Presidential election (October 2017) - relatively covered by independent media. Babanov vs. Jeenbekov campaign covered. Problematic environment but competitive election covered.

2018.assessment: RSF ~98 - decline. Atambaev-Jeenbekov feud creates media polarization. Independent journalism squeezed between factional propaganda. Babanov forced to leave country. Media environment deteriorating. Difficult.

2019.assessment: RSF ~83. Atambaev compound standoff (August 2019) - journalists covering event face risks. Atambaev arrested. Media polarized. Kloop.kg, Azattyk continue independent coverage. Problematic environment.

2020.assessment: RSF ~83. October 2020 revolution - journalists covering protests active. COVID restrictions limit some coverage earlier in year. Post-revolution: media relatively pluralistic if chaotic. Problematic environment.

2021.assessment: RSF ~79. Japarov wins presidency. Constitutional referendum (April 2021). Japarov nationalist rhetoric against foreign agents. Some independent media operating. Problematic environment.

2022.assessment: RSF 2022 (new methodology): Kyrgyzstan ~72. NGO and media foreign agents law (2022) threatens outlets receiving foreign funding. RSF notes increasing government pressure. Kloop.kg website blocked temporarily. Kyrgyz-Tajik conflict - media access restricted. Problematic.

2023.assessment: RSF 2023: ~66. Continued decline. Foreign agents law enforcement targeting media organizations. Kloop.kg and other outlets facing pressure. Online harassment of journalists. Japarov government increasingly intolerant of critical coverage. Problematic.

2024.assessment: RSF 2024: ~85 - decline. Kloop.kg (major independent outlet) blocked. Journalists prosecuted under new media laws. Foreign agents law actively applied. RSF notes Kyrgyzstan falling from Central Asia relative leader to increasingly difficult environment. Problematic to difficult.

2025.assessment: Estimated difficult environment. Continuing decline from 2024 problematic status. Kloop.kg blocked; foreign agents law limiting civil society monitoring. Independent online media reduced. Japarov consolidation restricting press freedom.
